subject: History HL
prompt: Explain the causes of Japanese expansion in Asia between 1931 and 1937.
markScheme:
Economic causes:
• Japan lacked domestic raw materials (oil, iron ore, rubber) — imperial expansion offered access to these resources [1]
• Great Depression (1929) devastated Japan's export economy and caused severe domestic political instability [1]

Political/military causes:
• Rise of ultranationalist and militarist factions; Kwantung Army acted autonomously of civilian government in staging Mukden Incident [1]
• Civilian government too weak to control military; assassinations (e.g. Prime Minister Inukai, 1932) eliminated moderate opposition [1]
• Ideology of pan-Asian leadership ('Hakko Ichiu' — 'eight corners of the world under one roof') and racial destiny [1]

International context:
• League of Nations' failure — Lytton Commission report delayed; no sanctions imposed; Japan simply withdrew from League in 1933 [1]
• Western powers distracted by Depression and European instability; US isolationism limited response [1]
• Success of Manchukuo puppet state (1932) emboldened further ambitions in northern China [1]
Max 8 marks

subject: History HL
prompt: Compare and contrast the methods used by Martin Luther King Jr and Malcolm X to advance African American civil rights in the 1960s.
markScheme:
Similarities:
• Both challenged white supremacy and the systematic inequality faced by African Americans [1]
• Both were powerful public orators who galvanised large followings [1]
• Both evolved in their views: King became more radical (Poor People's Campaign, anti-Vietnam); Malcolm X moderated after his pilgrimage to Mecca [1]

Differences — Methods:
• King: nonviolent direct action — sit-ins, marches, boycotts modelled on Gandhian satyagraha; sought to provoke moral crisis [1]
• Malcolm X: advocated self-defence ('by any means necessary'); rejected nonviolence as disarming Black people [1]

Differences — Goals:
• King sought racial integration within American society; Malcolm X (especially early) sought Black separatism and self-sufficiency [1]
• King worked with white liberals and the federal government; Malcolm X built independent Black institutions [1]

Broader significance:
• Their differences reflected deeper divisions in Black America between integration and separatism, and between reform and revolution [1]
Max 8 marks

subject: History HL

subject: History HL
prompt: "The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was the most significant achievement of the US Civil Rights Movement." To what extent do you agree with this assessment?
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15). Level 5 (13–15): clear analytical argument, specific evidence, evaluates multiple factors, substantiated conclusion.

Arguments FOR:
• Outlawed discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex or national origin in employment (Title VII) and public accommodations (Title II)
• Created EEOC as enforcement mechanism
• Gave federal government power to withhold funds from discriminatory programmes
• Culminated years of protest: sit-ins, Freedom Rides, Birmingham campaign, March on Washington

Arguments AGAINST / alternative achievements:
• Voting Rights Act (1965) arguably more transformative — Black voter registration in Mississippi rose from 6.7% to 59.8% within three years
• Brown v Board (1954) provided constitutional foundation: overturned Plessy v Ferguson, delegitimised Jim Crow
• Montgomery Bus Boycott demonstrated power of mass economic direct action
• Limitations: Civil Rights Act did not address housing discrimination (Fair Housing Act 1968), structural poverty, or Northern de facto segregation
• Black Power movement argued legal equality was insufficient without economic transformation

Conclusion must weigh evidence and arrive at a clear, substantiated judgement.

subject: History HL
prompt: Evaluate the role of appeasement in causing the Second World War in Europe.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Appeasement as cause:
• Munich 1938 confirmed Hitler's conviction that Western powers lacked will to fight — emboldened him to occupy all of Czechoslovakia in March 1939
• Each concession (Rhineland 1936, Anschluss March 1938, Sudetenland September 1938) raised Hitler's demands
• Surrendering Czech defences and Skoda armaments industry materially weakened Allied strategic position
• Failed to build collective security; excluded USSR, pushing Stalin toward the Nazi-Soviet Pact
• Chamberlain's 'peace in our time' gave Hitler time to complete rearmament on his terms

Appeasement not the primary cause:
• Hitler's expansionist ideology (Mein Kampf, Hossbach Memorandum 1937) suggests war was planned regardless
• A.J.P. Taylor argued appeasement was rational given Allied unpreparedness; war might have been worse in 1938
• Nazi-Soviet Pact (August 1939) enabled invasion of Poland — Soviet foreign policy also a cause
• American isolationism and weakness of League of Nations were structural factors beyond British/French control
• Some historians argue appeasement bought Britain crucial time for RAF rearmament (Battle of Britain, 1940)

Level 5 (13–15): sustained analytical evaluation, engages with historiographical debate, clear and well-supported conclusion on relative weight of appeasement.

subject: History HL
prompt: Compare and contrast Japanese and German expansion in the 1930s, with reference to their methods, motivations, and the international responses they provoked.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 3 markbands (0–15).

Similarities:
• Both driven by economic needs and imperial ideology — Japan needed raw materials; Germany pursued Lebensraum [1–2]
• Both exploited weaknesses of the international system — League of Nations failed to stop either power [1]
• Both used military force incrementally, testing Western resolve before escalating [1]
• Both driven by extreme nationalist ideology — Japanese ultramilitarism and pan-Asianism; German National Socialism [1]
• Both established puppet or client states (Manchukuo; Bohemia-Moravia protectorate) [1]

Differences:
• Germany's expansion was racially ideological (Aryan supremacy, anti-Semitism); Japanese expansion framed in pan-Asian, anti-colonial terms [1]
• German targets were European neighbours with ethnic German populations; Japan expanded into China and Southeast Asia [1]
• German expansion followed Hitler's deliberate sequence; Japanese military often acted autonomously of civilian government [1]
• Western powers ultimately declared war on Germany over Poland; Japan faced less organised Western resistance until Pearl Harbor (1941) [1]
• Scale of international reaction: League condemned Japan over Manchuria but imposed no effective sanctions; Munich showed even greater Western passivity toward Germany [1]

Level 5 (13–15): sophisticated comparison using specific evidence from both cases; reaches clear analytical conclusion about similarities and differences.

Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Other significant factors:

• Legal strategy — NAACP's court litigation (Brown v Board, Browder v Gayle) changed constitutional landscape
• Federal government role — Kennedy and especially Johnson used presidential authority and personal commitment
• Economic factors — Black consumer power; Northern Black vote becoming electorally significant
• Pressure from more radical movements (SNCC radicalisation, Black Power) created incentive for moderates to pass legislation quickly
• International context — decolonisation made US racial practices a global embarrassment

Level 5 (13–15): evaluates multiple factors with analytical depth, uses specific evidence, argues clearly for relative importance of nonviolent direct action vs other factors, substantiated conclusion.

subject: Mathematics AA
prompt:
Let f(x) = (3x − 1)/(x + 2), where x ≠ −2.

(a) Find f⁻¹(x), the inverse function of f. [3 marks]
(b) Write down the domain and range of f⁻¹(x). [2 marks]
(c) Verify your answer by showing f(f⁻¹(x)) = x. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) Let y = (3x − 1)/(x + 2). Swap x and y: x = (3y − 1)/(y + 2). Solve for y:
x(y + 2) = 3y − 1
xy + 2x = 3y − 1
2x + 1 = 3y − xy = y(3 − x)
y = (2x + 1)/(3 − x)
So f⁻¹(x) = (2x + 1)/(3 − x) [3 marks: 1 for swap, 1 for rearrangement, 1 for correct answer]

(b) Domain of f⁻¹: x ≠ 3 (denominator cannot be zero) [1]
    Range of f⁻¹: f⁻¹(x) ≠ −2 (equals domain restriction of f) [1]

(c) f(f⁻¹(x)) = f((2x+1)/(3−x)) = [3·(2x+1)/(3−x) − 1] / [(2x+1)/(3−x) + 2]
= [(6x+3−(3−x))/(3−x)] / [(2x+1+2(3−x))/(3−x)]
= (7x)/(7) = x ✓ [1]

subject: Mathematics AA
prompt:
A curve has equation y = x³ − 6x² + 9x + 2.

(a) Find dy/dx. [2 marks]
(b) Find the coordinates of the stationary points of the curve. [3 marks]
(c) Determine the nature of each stationary point. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) dy/dx = 3x² − 12x + 9 [2 marks: 1 for correct differentiation of each term]

(b) Set dy/dx = 0:
3x² − 12x + 9 = 0
x² − 4x + 3 = 0
(x − 1)(x − 3) = 0
x = 1 or x = 3 [1 for factorising/solving]
At x = 1: y = 1 − 6 + 9 + 2 = 6 → point (1, 6) [1]
At x = 3: y = 27 − 54 + 27 + 2 = 2 → point (3, 2) [1]

(c) d²y/dx² = 6x − 12
At x = 1: d²y/dx² = −6 < 0 → local maximum [1]
At x = 3: d²y/dx² = 6 > 0 → local minimum [1]

subject: Mathematics AA
prompt:
A company's profit P (in thousands of dollars) is modelled by the function:
P(x) = −2x³ + 15x² − 36x + 27,
where x is the number of units produced (in thousands), for 0 ≤ x ≤ 5.

(a) Find P'(x) and hence find all values of x at which the profit has a stationary point. [4 marks]
(b) Determine the maximum profit and the production level at which it occurs. [3 marks]
(c) Find the value of x for which the profit is increasing most rapidly (i.e. P'(x) is maximised). [3 marks]
(d) Sketch the shape of the profit curve, indicating stationary points and the nature of each. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) P'(x) = −6x² + 30x − 36 [1]
Set P'(x) = 0: −6x² + 30x − 36 = 0 → x² − 5x + 6 = 0 → (x−2)(x−3) = 0 [1]
x = 2 and x = 3 [1 each, total 1 for both]

(b) P''(x) = −12x + 30
At x = 2: P''(2) = −24 + 30 = 6 > 0 → local minimum
At x = 3: P''(3) = −36 + 30 = −6 < 0 → local maximum [1]
P(3) = −2(27) + 15(9) − 36(3) + 27 = −54 + 135 − 108 + 27 = 0 [1]
Also check endpoint P(0) = 27, P(5) = −250 + 375 − 180 + 27 = −28
Maximum profit is 27 thousand dollars at x = 0 or at local max check: maximum on [0,5] is P(0) = 27 [1]

(c) To maximise P'(x) = −6x² + 30x − 36, find P''(x) = 0:
−12x + 30 = 0 → x = 2.5 [2]
P'(2.5) = −6(6.25) + 30(2.5) − 36 = −37.5 + 75 − 36 = 1.5 [1]

(d) Award marks for: correct shape (cubic), local min at x=2, local max at x=3 marked, endpoints labelled [2]

subject: Mathematics AA
prompt:
A researcher tests whether a new teaching method improves exam scores. A random sample of 200 students taught with the new method has a mean score of 72.4 marks with standard deviation 9.6 marks. The national mean with the standard method is known to be μ₀ = 70 marks.

(a) State the null and alternative hypotheses for a one-tailed test. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the test statistic z. [2 marks]
(c) At the 5% significance level, determine whether there is sufficient evidence to conclude the new method is more effective. (Critical value z* = 1.645) [2 marks]
(d) Calculate the p-value and interpret its meaning in context. [2 marks]
(e) State one assumption required for this test to be valid. [1 mark]
(f) Describe a Type I error in this context. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) H₀: μ = 70 (no improvement); H₁: μ > 70 (new method improves scores) [2]

(b) z = (x̄ − μ₀)/(σ/√n) = (72.4 − 70)/(9.6/√200) = 2.4/(9.6/14.14) = 2.4/0.679 ≈ 3.535 [2: 1 for formula, 1 for correct calculation]

(c) Since z = 3.535 > z* = 1.645, we reject H₀ at 5% significance level. There is sufficient evidence that the new method improves scores. [2]

(d) p-value = P(Z > 3.535) ≈ 0.0002. This means there is only a 0.02% probability of obtaining a sample mean this high (or higher) if H₀ were true. Very strong evidence against H₀. [2]

(e) The sample is drawn randomly from the population OR the scores are approximately normally distributed OR the population standard deviation is known [1]

(f) A Type I error would be concluding the new teaching method improves scores when in fact it does not (rejecting a true null hypothesis). [1]

subject: Physics SL
prompt: A wave has a frequency of 500 Hz and travels at 340 m s⁻¹. What is its wavelength?

subject: Physics SL
prompt: An ideal gas occupies 2.0 m³ at a pressure of 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa and temperature 300 K. The gas is compressed to 1.0 m³ at the same temperature. What is the new pressure?
markScheme: C — Isothermal process: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. (1.0 × 10⁵)(2.0) = P₂(1.0) → P₂ = 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa.

subject: Physics SL
prompt: In the photoelectric effect, increasing the intensity of light (at constant frequency above the threshold) will:
markScheme: C — Intensity is proportional to the number of photons per second. Each photon can eject one electron (if it has enough energy), so more photons → more electrons emitted per second. The maximum kinetic energy depends only on frequency, not intensity.

choices.A: Increase the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons
choices.B: Increase the frequency of the emitted electrons
choices.C: Increase the number of electrons emitted per second

subject: Physics SL
prompt: A 2 kg object moving at 6 m s⁻¹ collides with and sticks to a stationary 4 kg object. What is the velocity of the combined mass after the collision?
markScheme: C — Conservation of momentum: p_before = (2)(6) = 12 kg m s⁻¹. p_after = (2+4)v = 6v. So v = 12/6 = 2 m s⁻¹.

choices.A: 6 m s⁻¹
choices.B: 3 m s⁻¹
choices.C: 2 m s⁻¹
choices.D: 4 m s⁻¹

subject: Physics SL
prompt: The Doppler effect causes a fire engine siren to appear to have a higher pitch as it approaches an observer. This is because:
markScheme: C — As the source moves toward the observer, successive wavefronts are emitted closer together (compressed wavelength). Since v = fλ and wave speed is constant in the medium, a shorter wavelength means higher observed frequency.

choices.A: The speed of sound increases as the source moves toward the observer
choices.B: The amplitude of the sound waves increases
choices.C: The wavelength of sound received by the observer is compressed, increasing the observed frequency
choices.D: The fire engine produces more sound energy when moving toward the observer

subject: Physics SL
prompt: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 6 hours. What fraction of the original sample remains after 24 hours?
markScheme: C — Number of half-lives in 24 hours = 24/6 = 4. Fraction remaining = (1/2)⁴ = 1/16.

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A projectile is launched from the ground at an angle of 30° above the horizontal with an initial speed of 40 m s⁻¹. Take g = 10 m s⁻² and ignore air resistance.

(a) Calculate the horizontal and vertical components of the initial velocity. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the time of flight (total time in the air). [2 marks]
(c) Calculate the horizontal range of the projectile. [2 marks]
(d) Calculate the maximum height reached. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) uₓ = u cos30° = 40 × (√3/2) = 40 × 0.866 = 34.6 m s⁻¹ [1]
    uᵧ = u sin30° = 40 × 0.5 = 20 m s⁻¹ [1]

(b) Time to maximum height: uᵧ = gt_max → t_max = 20/10 = 2 s [1]
    Total time of flight = 2 × t_max = 4 s [1]

(c) Range = uₓ × t_total = 34.6 × 4 = 138.4 m ≈ 138 m [2]
    (Accept use of Range = u²sin(2θ)/g = 1600×sin60°/10 = 160×0.866 = 138.6 m)

(d) h_max = uᵧ²/(2g) = 400/20 = 20 m [1]

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A circuit consists of a 12 V battery (internal resistance 0.5 Ω) connected to an external circuit. The external circuit has two resistors: R₁ = 3 Ω connected in series with a parallel combination of R₂ = 4 Ω and R₃ = 12 Ω.

(a) Calculate the combined resistance of R₂ and R₃ in parallel. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the total resistance of the circuit including internal resistance. [2 marks]
(c) Calculate the current through the battery. [1 mark]
(d) Calculate the terminal voltage of the battery. [1 mark]
(e) Calculate the power dissipated in R₁. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) 1/R₂₃ = 1/4 + 1/12 = 3/12 + 1/12 = 4/12 → R₂₃ = 3 Ω [2]

(b) R_external = R₁ + R₂₃ = 3 + 3 = 6 Ω
    R_total = R_external + r = 6 + 0.5 = 6.5 Ω [2]

(c) I = EMF/R_total = 12/6.5 ≈ 1.85 A [1]

(d) V_terminal = EMF − Ir = 12 − 1.85 × 0.5 = 12 − 0.92 ≈ 11.1 V [1]

(e) P_R₁ = I²R₁ = (1.85)² × 3 ≈ 3.42 × 3 ≈ 10.3 W [2: 1 for formula, 1 for answer]

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A sample of 0.50 mol of an ideal gas is held in a container at a temperature of 27°C and a pressure of 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa. The universal gas constant R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

(a) Calculate the volume of the gas. [2 marks]
(b) The gas is heated at constant pressure until its temperature doubles. Calculate the new volume. [2 marks]
(c) Calculate the work done by the gas during this expansion. [2 marks]
(d) State the first law of thermodynamics and explain what it means for this process. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) PV = nRT; V = nRT/P = (0.50)(8.31)(300) / (1.0 × 10⁵)
    V = 1246.5 / 100000 = 0.01247 m³ ≈ 1.25 × 10⁻² m³ [2]

(b) At constant pressure (isobaric): V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
    New T = 600 K; V₂ = V₁ × (T₂/T₁) = 1.25 × 10⁻² × (600/300) = 2.50 × 10⁻² m³ [2]

(c) W = PΔV = P(V₂ − V₁) = (1.0 × 10⁵)(2.50 × 10⁻² − 1.25 × 10⁻²)
    W = (1.0 × 10⁵)(1.25 × 10⁻²) = 1250 J ≈ 1.25 × 10³ J [2]

(d) First law: ΔU = Q − W. The internal energy of the gas increases because the temperature increases. Heat is supplied (Q > 0) and some is used to do work (W > 0). [1]

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
The photoelectric effect is investigated using a metal with a work function φ = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Light of frequency 8.0 × 10¹⁴ Hz is incident on the metal surface. (Planck's constant h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s)

(a) Calculate the energy of a single photon of this light. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of an emitted electron. [2 marks]
(c) Explain why electrons are not emitted if the frequency of light is below the threshold frequency. [2 marks]
(d) Calculate the threshold frequency for this metal. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) E = hf = (6.63 × 10⁻³⁴)(8.0 × 10¹⁴) = 5.304 × 10⁻¹⁹ J ≈ 5.30 × 10⁻¹⁹ J [2]

(b) KE_max = E − φ = 5.30 × 10⁻¹⁹ − 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 2.10 × 10⁻¹⁹ J [2]

(c) Each photon transfers a fixed amount of energy E = hf. If this energy is less than the work function φ (minimum energy needed to remove an electron from the metal surface), no electron can be emitted regardless of intensity. Increasing intensity only increases number of photons, not individual photon energy. [2]

(d) At threshold: hf₀ = φ → f₀ = φ/h = (3.2 × 10⁻¹⁹)/(6.63 × 10⁻³⁴) ≈ 4.83 × 10¹⁴ Hz [1]

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A car of mass 1200 kg is moving at 25 m s⁻¹ when the driver applies the brakes. The car decelerates uniformly and comes to rest after travelling 62.5 m.

(a) Calculate the deceleration of the car. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the braking force applied to the car. [2 marks]
(c) Calculate the kinetic energy lost during braking, and state where this energy goes. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) v² = u² + 2as: 0 = (25)² + 2a(62.5)
    2a(62.5) = −625 → a = −5 m s⁻² (deceleration = 5 m s⁻²) [2]

(b) F = ma = 1200 × 5 = 6000 N [2]

(c) ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu² = 0 − ½(1200)(25²) = −375,000 J = −375 kJ [1]
    Energy is converted primarily into thermal energy (heat) in the brake pads and discs, and some into sound. [1]

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A student sets up a Young's double-slit experiment using a monochromatic light source with wavelength 600 nm. The slit separation is 0.2 mm and the screen is placed 1.5 m from the slits.

(a) Calculate the fringe spacing (distance between adjacent bright fringes) on the screen. [3 marks]
(b) Describe what would be observed on the screen and explain the physical reason for the pattern. [3 marks]
(c) The student replaces the monochromatic source with white light. Describe and explain the differences in the observed pattern. [2 marks]
(d) The slit separation is then doubled. Describe the effect on the fringe spacing and explain why. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) Fringe spacing: y = λD/d = (600 × 10⁻⁹ × 1.5) / (0.2 × 10⁻³)
    y = (9 × 10⁻⁷) / (2 × 10⁻⁴) = 4.5 × 10⁻³ m = 4.5 mm [3: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer]

(b) A series of alternating bright and dark fringes (interference pattern) equally spaced across the screen [1]. Bright fringes occur where path difference = nλ (constructive interference — waves arrive in phase) [1]. Dark fringes occur where path difference = (n + ½)λ (destructive interference — waves arrive out of phase and cancel) [1].

(c) Each wavelength in white light produces its own fringe pattern with different spacing [1]. The central fringe is white, but fringes either side appear as spectra (violet closest to centre, red furthest out), since fringe spacing ∝ wavelength. Fringes rapidly overlap and appear as a smeared white pattern away from centre [1].

(d) Fringe spacing halves [1]. Since y = λD/d, doubling d halves y. The fringes become more closely spaced because the angular separation between paths from the two slits to any point is greater [1].

subject: Physics SL
prompt:
A block of ice at 0°C is heated until all the ice has melted and the water has reached 80°C. The mass of the ice is 0.50 kg. Specific heat capacity of water: c = 4200 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹; Specific latent heat of fusion of ice: L = 3.34 × 10⁵ J kg⁻¹.

(a) Calculate the energy required to melt all the ice at 0°C. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the additional energy required to heat the water from 0°C to 80°C. [2 marks]
(c) Calculate the total energy supplied. [1 mark]
(d) Explain, at the molecular level, why energy must be supplied to melt ice even though the temperature does not change during melting. [3 marks]
(e) Sketch a temperature-time graph for this entire heating process, labelling all important features. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) Q₁ = mL = 0.50 × 3.34 × 10⁵ = 1.67 × 10⁵ J = 167,000 J [2]

(b) Q₂ = mcΔT = 0.50 × 4200 × 80 = 168,000 J [2]

(c) Q_total = Q₁ + Q₂ = 167,000 + 168,000 = 335,000 J = 3.35 × 10⁵ J [1]

(d) In ice, water molecules are held in a regular crystal lattice by hydrogen bonds [1]. Energy is needed to break these intermolecular bonds (potential energy increases) rather than to increase the kinetic energy of the molecules [1]. Since temperature is related to average kinetic energy of molecules, and kinetic energy is not increasing during melting, the temperature remains constant at 0°C despite energy being continuously supplied [1].

(e) Graph should show: rising line (if ice heated from below 0°C — accept if started at 0°C); horizontal plateau at 0°C during melting [1]; rising line from 0°C to 80°C after melting complete; labelled axes (temperature/°C on y-axis, time on x-axis) [1].

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "The quality of knowledge is best measured by how many people believe it." Discuss this claim with reference to two areas of knowledge.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define 'quality of knowledge' — truth, reliability, certainty, usefulness? [1]
• Challenge the premise: popular belief does not determine truth (e.g. historical scientific consensus — heliocentrism, germ theory rejected before accepted) [1]
• AOK 1 — Natural Sciences: scientific knowledge is not determined by popular vote; peer review, reproducibility, predictive power are better measures [1–2]
• AOK 2 — Human Sciences or History: majority consensus in social science or historical interpretation can shift; does popularity improve or reflect quality? [1–2]
• Knowledge claim: Some knowledge genuinely depends on social agreement (e.g. ethics, laws, currencies) — here 'many people believing' may partly constitute the knowledge [1]
• Counterclaim: Flat Earth belief held by millions — clearly does not improve quality; quality must be independent of popularity [1]
• Real-life situation: e.g. climate science — 97% scientific consensus vs. popular scepticism; shows that expert consensus differs from popular belief [1]
• Nuanced conclusion [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Language itself shapes what we can know." To what extent do you agree with this statement?
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define terms: 'language' (natural language, scientific notation, artistic forms); 'shapes what we can know' (determines vs. influences) [1]
• Knowledge claim: Sapir-Whorf hypothesis — strong version: language determines thought; weak version: language influences perception [1]
• Example: Colour perception — speakers of languages with more colour terms perceive distinctions more readily (e.g. Russian speakers and blue-green distinction) [1]
• AOK — Natural Sciences: scientific language (mathematical notation) enables knowledge that would be impossible in ordinary language; physics of quantum mechanics expressible only in mathematics [1]
• AOK — Mathematics: mathematical language creates its own knowledge domain — concepts like infinity, imaginary numbers [1]
• Counterclaim: Translation between languages preserves meaning; people can think thoughts for which they lack words (pre-linguistic thought); animals with no language have spatial knowledge [1]
• Real-life situation: deaf communities and sign language — knowledge is constructed and communicated differently but not necessarily limited [1]
• Nuanced conclusion: language shapes but does not wholly determine knowledge [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Artificial intelligence can produce knowledge, but it cannot understand it." Discuss this claim with reference to at least two ways of knowing.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define 'knowledge' and 'understanding' — what distinguishes them? [1]
• Knowledge claim: AI systems (e.g. large language models) produce outputs indistinguishable from human knowledge claims, solve mathematical problems, generate scientific hypotheses [1]
• Way of Knowing 1 — Reason: AI appears to reason logically, but critics argue it performs pattern matching without intentionality (Searle's Chinese Room) [1–2]
• Way of Knowing 2 — Intuition/Emotion: AI lacks genuine experience, emotional investment, or intuitive leaps rooted in embodied existence [1]
• AOK — Natural Sciences: AI has discovered novel scientific results (AlphaFold, protein structure); does discovery imply understanding? [1]
• AOK — Arts: AI generates creative works but arguably without genuine aesthetic experience or intention [1]
• Counterclaim: 'Understanding' is philosophically contested — functionalists argue if the outputs are correct, the distinction doesn't matter [1]
• Real-life situation: AI medical diagnosis — can identify cancer in scans better than human radiologists; does this constitute 'knowing'? [1]
• Conclusion: AI produces knowledge claims but lacks phenomenal understanding; the distinction matters epistemologically even if not practically [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Those in positions of power have the greatest influence over what counts as knowledge." Evaluate this claim.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define 'power' and 'what counts as knowledge' — gatekeeping, agenda-setting, legitimisation [1]
• Knowledge claim: Foucault's power/knowledge — knowledge is not neutral; powerful institutions (governments, universities, media) define legitimate knowledge [1]
• Real-life situation 1: Colonial epistemologies — indigenous knowledge systems marginalised and dismissed as superstition by colonial powers [1]
• Real-life situation 2: Soviet Lysenkoist biology — politically mandated pseudoscience; geneticists persecuted; shows power can enforce false knowledge claims [1]
• AOK — History: who writes the historical record? Victors shape what is 'known' about the past [1]
• AOK — Natural Sciences: peer-review and academic funding controlled by institutions; 'paradigm shifts' (Kuhn) resist established power [1]
• Counterclaim: Scientific method provides some independence from power; reproducibility and falsifiability as checks; whistleblowers, independent media [1]
• Nuanced conclusion: power shapes what is socially legitimised as knowledge, but cannot indefinitely suppress true claims — reality constrains power's reach [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Does it matter if our knowledge is true?" Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Interrogate what 'matters' means — practically, morally, epistemologically [1]
• Knowledge claim: truth matters because false beliefs lead to bad decisions — medical, scientific, ethical examples [1]
• AOK 1 — Natural Sciences: truth matters enormously — engineering bridges or designing vaccines on false knowledge leads to material harm [1–2]
• AOK 2 — Ethics: if moral beliefs are false (e.g. belief that a group is inferior justifies discrimination), the consequences are catastrophic [1–2]
• Counterclaim: pragmatism — William James: true is what works; some beliefs are useful even if not strictly 'true' (placebo effect; religious belief supporting wellbeing) [1]
• AOK — Arts: fictional narratives contain no factual truths but generate profound understanding of human experience [1]
• Real-life situation: climate denial — the social and political cost of not accepting the truth about climate change is existential [1]
• Nuanced conclusion: truth matters profoundly in domains where beliefs guide action; pragmatic value of 'useful fictions' has limits [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Our personal experiences shape what we know, but they can also distort it." Discuss this claim with reference to memory and sense perception as ways of knowing.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Acknowledge productive tension: experience is necessary for knowledge yet introduces subjectivity [1]
• WOK 1 — Memory: episodic memory is the basis of historical personal knowledge; research (Loftus) shows memory is reconstructive, not reproductive; false memory syndrome [1–2]
• WOK 2 — Sense Perception: our senses provide primary data about the world; but optical illusions, selective attention, confirmation bias show perception is theory-laden [1–2]
• AOK — History: individual testimony (eyewitness accounts) both essential and unreliable [1]
• AOK — Human Sciences: observer effect; researcher's personal experience shapes qualitative research findings [1]
• Knowledge claim: without personal experience, knowledge would be purely abstract and lack grounding [1]
• Real-life situation: trauma and memory — PTSD shows how extreme experience can both intensify and distort recall of events [1]
• Conclusion: personal experience is epistemically necessary but must be checked by other sources; intersubjective verification matters [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: Explain, with one real-life example, how technology can both expand and restrict access to knowledge.
markScheme:
Expand access:
• Internet/Wikipedia makes vast amounts of information freely available globally [1]
• Open-access publishing removes paywalls; scientific knowledge democratised [1]
• Real-life example: e.g. MIT OpenCourseWare allows free access to university education worldwide [1]

Restrict access:
• Algorithmic filter bubbles restrict what users see, creating knowledge silos [1]
• Internet censorship (e.g. China's Great Firewall) restricts what knowledge is accessible [1]
• Digital divide — technology access requires wealth; global inequality limits who benefits [1]
Max 6 marks (3 for expansion, 3 for restriction, with at least one real-life example)

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: Explain how the use of metaphor in language can both illuminate and mislead in the construction of knowledge. Use one example from science or politics.
markScheme:
Illuminate:
• Metaphor makes abstract concepts accessible — 'electric current' uses water flow metaphor to explain electricity [1]
• Enables new connections between domains — 'natural selection' metaphor from artificial breeding illuminated evolutionary theory [1]

Mislead:
• Metaphors import unexamined assumptions — calling the brain a 'computer' implies aspects of mind that may not apply [1]
• Political metaphors: 'war on drugs' implies military solutions to a public health problem; shapes policy thinking harmfully [1]

Example development (science or politics):
• Scientific: 'selfish gene' (Dawkins) — illuminates evolutionary logic but misleads by implying intentionality in DNA [1]
• Political: 'immigrants as flood/wave' — normalises dehumanising framing [1]
Max 6 marks

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: TOK Exhibition prompt: 'What is the relationship between knowledge and politics?' Using one object or real-world example, describe how political context shapes the production or reception of knowledge.
markScheme:
Object/example identification — must be specific and concrete [1]
Examples accepted:
• A scientific report commissioned by a government [1] — shaped by funding priorities or suppressed if inconvenient [1]
• A history textbook — national curriculum shapes which historical narratives are taught as 'knowledge' [1]
• A propaganda poster — illustrates how political power produces competing knowledge claims [1]
• Social media content moderation policies — political context determines what 'misinformation' means [1]

Connection to TOK concepts:
• Must discuss how political context does not just surround knowledge but actively shapes what is produced, accepted, or dismissed [1]
Max 6 marks

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: Identify two ways of knowing and explain how each contributes differently to knowledge in the natural sciences.
markScheme:
For each WOK: [1] identify, [1] explain contribution to Natural Sciences, [1] example

Reason:
• Deductive and inductive reasoning enables scientists to form hypotheses and draw general conclusions from specific observations [1]
• E.g. Kepler used mathematical reasoning to derive laws of planetary motion [1]

Sense Perception:
• Empirical observation provides primary data — the foundation of experimental science [1]
• E.g. Galileo's telescopic observations challenged geocentric model [1]

Other accepted WOKs (imagination, intuition, emotion) with relevant scientific examples
Max 6 marks

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: Explain how bias — whether conscious or unconscious — can affect the production of knowledge in the human sciences.
markScheme:
Definition of bias in this context:
• A systematic tendency to favour certain conclusions, often without awareness [1]

Conscious bias:
• Researcher selects data that confirms pre-existing theory; cherry-picking results; publication bias (only positive results published) [1]

Unconscious bias:
• Observer effect: researcher's presence changes participants' behaviour; demand characteristics in experiments [1]
• Implicit bias: e.g. gender bias in psychology studies that used only male subjects and generalised results to all humans [1]

Real-life example:
• Replication crisis in psychology (Bem's ESP study; Stapel fraud) demonstrates how motivated reasoning corrupts knowledge [1]
• Tuskegee Syphilis Study — researchers' racial bias affected ethical and epistemic decisions [1]
Max 6 marks

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: TOK Exhibition IA Prompt: 'Is there knowledge that should never be sought?' Evaluate this prompt using one concrete object or real-world situation.
markScheme:
Object must be concrete and linked to a genuine knowledge question [1]

Knowledge claim — some knowledge should not be sought:
• Research into engineering more lethal pathogens (gain-of-function research) — knowing how to make a deadlier virus may create existential risk [1]
• Psychological torture research — Milgram obedience experiments raised ethical concerns even if knowledge gained was valuable [1]

Counterclaim — restricting knowledge is dangerous:
• Censorship of research has historically been used to suppress inconvenient truths (e.g. tobacco industry suppressing cancer links) [1]
• Scientific progress often requires exploring ethically uncomfortable questions [1]

TOK concepts applied:
• Knowledge and its consequences; responsibility of the knower; distinction between seeking and applying knowledge [1]
• Who decides what should not be sought, and how? Power and politics [1]

Conclusion: nuanced evaluation of whether limits on knowledge-seeking are ever justified [1]
Max 8 marks

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "History is written by the victors." Discuss this claim with reference to history as an area of knowledge and at least one other area of knowledge.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Unpack the claim: powerful nations/groups shape historical narratives; marginalised voices are excluded [1]
• AOK — History: colonial histories written from European perspective; indigenous accounts suppressed (e.g. North American history, African colonial history) [1–2]
• WOK — Language: the language of historical record (who writes, in what language) shapes what is preserved [1]
• AOK 2 — Human Sciences: sociology of knowledge — Foucault's archive — what gets documented and why [1]
• Knowledge claim: primary sources, archaeology, oral traditions provide partial counter-narratives [1]
• Real-life situation: reinterpretation of WWII in Japan vs. China/Korea — same events, radically different national histories [1]
• Counterclaim: modern historiography increasingly gives voice to marginalised groups; subaltern studies; digital preservation [1]
• Conclusion: there is truth in the claim, but it is not absolute — scholarship and activism can recover suppressed knowledge [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "Intuition is a more reliable source of knowledge in the arts than in the natural sciences." To what extent do you agree?
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define intuition — immediate understanding without conscious reasoning; expert intuition vs. gut feeling [1]
• AOK — Arts: artists rely on intuitive decisions about form, composition, emotional impact; aesthetic judgement is often immediate [1]
• Role of intuition in artistic creation vs. reception — both involve non-deliberate knowledge [1]
• AOK — Natural Sciences: scientists describe 'Eureka' moments; expert intuition guides hypothesis formation (Kary Mullis, PCR) [1]
• Counterclaim in Arts: intuition in arts must be cultivated through practice; not naive gut feeling — trained intuition [1]
• Counterclaim in Sciences: scientific method exists precisely because intuition is unreliable — cognitive biases, counterintuitive results (quantum mechanics) [1]
• Real-life comparison: mathematical intuition — mathematicians use intuition to select proof strategies, yet proof must be rigorous [1]
• Conclusion: intuition is important in both AOKs but is more socially accepted in Arts; in Sciences it must be verified; 'reliability' is domain-dependent [1]

subject: Theory of Knowledge
prompt: "The obligation to share knowledge can conflict with the right to privacy." Discuss this claim with reference to two areas of knowledge.
markScheme:
Strong responses will:
• Define both obligations: duty to share (scientific progress, public health) vs. right to privacy (personal, proprietary) [1]
• AOK 1 — Natural Sciences/Medicine: tension between open-data science and patient confidentiality; COVID-19 genome sharing vs. individual privacy [1–2]
• AOK 2 — Human Sciences: social science research using personal data (Facebook emotional contagion study) — did public benefit justify covert data use? [1–2]
• WOK — Emotion: strong emotional attachment to privacy; equally strong communal need for knowledge (contact tracing, disease surveillance) [1]
• Real-life situation: whistleblowers (Snowden, Deep Throat) — chose to share knowledge despite legal and personal risk; public interest vs. institutional secrecy [1]
• AOK — Ethics: consequentialist argument for sharing (maximise public benefit) vs. deontological argument for privacy (persons as ends, not means) [1]
• Conclusion: tension is genuine and context-dependent; no universal resolution — governance frameworks attempt to balance both [1]

subject: English B HL
prompt:
Read the following extract from a newspaper editorial:

"We have been told, time and again, that austerity is the only solution. The figures, we are assured, leave no room for doubt. And yet, when one examines the evidence dispassionately, a rather different picture emerges."

The tone of this passage is best described as:
markScheme: B — The writer uses hedging ('we are told', 'we are assured') to create ironic distance from official claims. 'And yet' introduces a counterargument; 'rather different picture' is understated criticism. The overall register is analytical but sceptical.

choices.A: Enthusiastic and supportive of government policy
choices.B: Sceptical and subtly critical of official claims
choices.C: Neutral and objective, presenting both sides equally

subject: English B HL
prompt: Which of the following best describes the purpose of a 'call to action' at the end of a fundraising email?

choices.A: To entertain the reader with a memorable conclusion
choices.B: To inform the reader about the organisation's history
choices.C: To persuade the reader to take a specific action such as donating or signing up

subject: English B HL
prompt: In George Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four, the Party slogan 'WAR IS PEACE. FREEDOM IS SLAVERY. IGNORANCE IS STRENGTH.' is an example of which literary device?

choices.C: Alliteration

subject: English B HL

subject: English B HL
prompt: In The Kite Runner by Khaled Hosseini, the kite serves primarily as a symbol of:

The act of kite running at the end echoes the novel's opening and signals Amir's emotional transformation.

choices.A: Afghan national pride and military resistance
choices.B: Amir's wealth and social status in Kabul

subject: English B HL
prompt:
Read the following extract from an opinion article about social media:

"Every morning, millions of us perform the same ritual: we reach for our phones before our feet touch the floor. We scroll. We swipe. We consume. What are we looking for? Connection? Validation? Or simply the next dose of outrage to power us through the day? Social media companies don't want you to ask these questions. They profit most when you are most numb."

Analyse how the writer uses language to convey their perspective on social media. Refer to specific techniques and their effects.
markScheme:
Award marks for identifying and analysing specific language techniques with comment on effect:

Techniques (identify + effect):
• 'The same ritual' — metaphor; frames phone-checking as quasi-religious compulsive behaviour, implies addiction [1–2]
• Tricolon with verbs: 'We scroll. We swipe. We consume.' — simple, staccato sentences mimic mechanical, mindless action; 'consume' has negative connotations [1–2]
• Rhetorical questions ('What are we looking for?') — engages reader, implicates them in the criticism [1]
• 'Next dose of outrage' — drug metaphor; presents social media as addictive, harmful substance; 'dose' has medical/pharmacological resonance [1–2]
• Direct address ('they don't want you to ask') — creates 'us vs. them' (users vs. corporations); conspiratorial register [1]
• 'Most numb' — ends on stark, bleak note; implies social media deadens emotional intelligence [1]
• Second person 'you' — directly implicates reader, increases persuasive impact [1]

Max 8 marks; credit integrated analysis (technique + effect + evaluation)

subject: English B HL
prompt: In Nineteen Eighty-Four by George Orwell, explain how the concept of 'Newspeak' functions as a tool of political control. Refer to specific examples from the novel.
markScheme:
Definition/explanation:
• Newspeak is the Party's deliberately constructed language designed to make thoughtcrime literally impossible [1]
• By eliminating words (and their associated concepts), the Party destroys the intellectual tools needed to think critically [1]

Specific functions:
• Words for political concepts such as 'freedom', 'rebellion', 'equality' are removed or redefined to make them un-thinkable [1]
• 'Crimestop' — the ability to stop a dangerous thought before it becomes conscious [1]
• Syme (Newspeak dictionary editor) explains that by 2050, Oldspeak (standard English) will be impossible; history will be untranslatable [1]

Effects on characters:
• Winston's work in the Ministry of Truth — rewriting historical records in Newspeak — shows how language destruction enables revisionism [1]
• Winston's diary is a subversive act precisely because he uses Oldspeak to articulate forbidden thoughts [1]

Broader literary/political significance:
• Orwell's essay 'Politics and the English Language' — political language is designed to make lies sound truthful; Newspeak is the extreme version [1]

Max 8 marks

subject: English B HL
prompt: In The Kite Runner, analyse how Hosseini uses the relationship between Amir and Hassan to explore themes of guilt and redemption. Support your answer with specific textual references.
markScheme:
Relationship overview:
• Amir (Pashtun, wealthy) and Hassan (Hazara, servant) — class and ethnic hierarchy shapes the relationship from the start [1]

Guilt:
• Amir witnesses Hassan's rape by Assef but does not intervene — act of cowardice that haunts him throughout [1]
• Amir then frames Hassan for theft to remove him from the house — doubles his betrayal [1]
• 'I thought about Hassan's harelip. I thought about that winter day in 1975. I thought about the alley.' — recurring motif of haunted memory [1]

Redemption:
• Amir returns to Taliban-controlled Afghanistan to rescue Sohrab (Hassan's son) — symbolic act of atonement [1]
• His willingness to be beaten by Assef mirrors Hassan's suffering — physical punishment as moral redemption [1]
• Final image: kite-running with Sohrab — 'For you, a thousand times over' echoes Hassan's loyalty; Amir becomes the kite runner for the next generation [1]

Thematic significance:
• Baba's secret paternity of Hassan: guilt is intergenerational; 'There is only one sin, only one. And that is theft.' [1]

Max 8 marks

subject: English B HL
prompt:
An advertisement for a luxury car shows a sleek silver vehicle on an empty mountain road at sunrise. The tagline reads: 'The world is yours. Own every moment.'

Describe and analyse how visual and linguistic elements work together to appeal to the target audience.
markScheme:
Visual elements:
• Empty road — connotations of exclusivity, freedom, lack of ordinary constraints [1]
• Sunrise — associations with new beginnings, opportunity, achievement [1]
• Silver/chrome — connotes prestige, modernity, high-technology [1]

Linguistic elements:
• 'The world is yours' — hyperbole; echoes of classical ownership and conquest; appeals to aspiration [1]
• 'Own every moment' — dual meaning: possess moments (time mastery) and own/drive (literal car purchase); imperative creates urgency [1]
• Alliteration/rhythm — short, punchy tagline for memorability [1]

Target audience:
• Affluent, aspirational adults; appeals to desire for status, freedom, and distinction from the ordinary [1]

Max 6 marks

subject: English B HL
prompt: Identify and explain six key features of an effective speech. For each feature, provide a brief example or explanation of its purpose.
markScheme:
Award 1 mark per feature with explanation (max 6):
• Direct address to audience ('Ladies and gentlemen', 'we') — creates rapport and engagement [1]
• Rhetorical questions — involves audience, stimulates thought [1]
• Rule of three / tricolon ('We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields') — rhythm and memorability [1]
• Anaphora (repetition at the start of clauses) — builds emotional momentum [1]
• Vivid imagery and specific examples — makes abstract arguments concrete and relatable [1]
• Clear structure (introduction, body, conclusion) with signposting — guides audience through argument [1]
• Emotive language — appeals to shared values and emotions [1]
• Varied sentence length — short sentences for impact; longer for explanation [1]
Max 6 marks

subject: English B HL
prompt:
A charity campaign poster reads:
"1 in 8 children goes to bed hungry tonight. Will you sleep soundly?"

Explain how specific language choices create an emotional response in the audience and encourage action.
markScheme:
• '1 in 8' — statistical credibility; makes abstract problem concrete and measurable [1]
• 'children' — appeals to protective instinct; children as innocent victims generate sympathy [1]
• 'goes to bed hungry' — euphemistic but vivid; 'bed' is intimate, domestic — creates uncomfortable juxtaposition [1]
• 'tonight' — immediacy; not a distant or abstract problem but happening right now [1]
• 'Will you sleep soundly?' — rhetorical question directly implicates reader; 'soundly' contrasts with hunger; implies moral discomfort if one does nothing [1]
• Second person ('you') — personal responsibility; cannot remain a bystander [1]
Max 6 marks

subject: English B HL
prompt: Write a formal speech (400–500 words) to be delivered at a school assembly, arguing that schools should reduce the use of smartphones during school hours. Your speech should engage your audience, present a clear argument, and use appropriate rhetorical devices.
markScheme:
Assess using IB English B HL Written Production criteria:

Language (0–10): accuracy, range, and appropriateness of vocabulary, grammar, register
• Level 5 (9–10): wide range of vocabulary and structures used accurately; register consistently formal and appropriate to speech context

Message (0–10): content, organisation, format adherence
• Level 5 (9–10): clear, convincing argument; structured with introduction, body, conclusion; rhetorical devices used effectively; audience engagement strategies present

Expected content:

• Appropriate formal register throughout

subject: English B HL
prompt: Your school's online magazine has asked for articles on the theme 'The world in 2050'. Write an article (400–500 words) exploring how artificial intelligence will change one aspect of daily life by 2050. Your article should be informative and engaging for a student audience.

Assess using IB English B HL Written Production criteria:

subject: English B HL
prompt: "Literature allows us to understand experiences and perspectives very different from our own." Discuss this statement with reference to one or two literary works you have studied.
markScheme:
Assess using IB English B HL Written Production criteria (Language 0–10; Message 0–10).

Expected content (reference to Nineteen Eighty-Four and/or The Kite Runner):

1984:
• Allows readers to understand life under totalitarianism — surveillance, psychological manipulation, loss of privacy and truth
• Winston's experience makes abstract political ideas visceral and personal

The Kite Runner:
• Allows non-Afghan readers to understand Afghan culture, class divisions (Pashtun/Hazara), impact of Soviet invasion, Taliban rule
• Amir's guilt and redemption are universal, but cultural specificity broadens empathy

Development:
• Literary techniques (characterisation, first-person narrative, imagery) enable identification with different experiences
• But: limit of literature — does it create genuine empathy or reinforce stereotypes? (Said's Orientalism)
• Reader's own experience shapes interpretation

Level 5 Language (9–10): sophisticated essay writing; varied structures; accurate grammar
Level 5 Message (9–10): clear thesis; detailed textual evidence; evaluative and reflective analysis; coherent structure

subject: English B HL
prompt:
Read the two short extracts and compare the way each writer presents their attitude to climate change.

Text A (blog post): "The science is settled, the debate is over, and every week we delay costs us decades of future suffering. Young people are inheriting a burning planet from the generation that chose comfort over conscience."

Text B (newspaper editorial): "While concern for the environment is entirely legitimate, we must be careful not to sacrifice economic growth on the altar of panic. Measured, evidence-based policy is preferable to the hysteria that currently dominates the conversation."
markScheme:
Text A:
• Urgent, emotive register — 'burning planet', 'suffering', 'chose comfort over conscience' [1]
• Accusatory tone toward older generation — intergenerational blame [1]
• Metaphor: 'burning planet' — apocalyptic imagery; 'altar of panic' used by B, but A uses 'burning' [1]

Text B:
• More measured, cautious register — 'entirely legitimate', 'measured, evidence-based' [1]
• Dismissive of activist urgency — 'panic', 'hysteria' — delegitimises alarmist discourse [1]
• Implicit pro-growth economic priority — 'sacrifice economic growth' — values balanced against environment [1]

Comparison:
• A presents climate action as moral imperative; B presents it as one priority among many [1]
• A uses emotional appeal; B uses appeals to reason and moderation [1]
• Both address policy but from opposite political/rhetorical positions [1]

Max 8 marks; credit integrated comparison with specific textual evidence

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:

que volaban entre las sombras."

¿Cuál recurso literario predomina en estos versos?

choices.B: La muerte y el destino fatal

markScheme: B — El blanco en la obra es irónico: representa la apariencia de pureza y el honor que Bernarda impone sobre sus hijas, mientras que en su interior se ocultan el deseo, la envidia y la desesperación. La pureza exterior contrasta violentamente con la realidad interior de represión.

choices.A: La pureza espiritual y la devoción religiosa sincera de la familia
choices.B: La inocencia reprimida y la apariencia social que oculta pasiones y opresión

choices.D: El luto oficial por la muerte del padre

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:
Lee el siguiente fragmento del poema «Puedo escribir los versos» de Pablo Neruda (Veinte poemas de amor y una canción desesperada) y realiza un comentario guiado:

«Puedo escribir los versos más tristes esta noche.
Escribo, por ejemplo: 'La noche está estrellada,
y tiritan, azules, los astros, a lo lejos.'»

(a) Identifica el tono del poema y justifica tu respuesta con referencia al fragmento. [2 puntos]
(b) Analiza el efecto del adverbio temporal 'esta noche' en el primer verso. [2 puntos]
(c) Comenta el uso de los colores y su función en la imagen poética del fragmento. [2 puntos]
(d) ¿Cómo contribuye la intertextualidad del verso entre comillas al significado del poema? [2 puntos]
markScheme:
(a) Tono elegíaco y melancólico, propio del amor perdido [1]. Justificación: 'los versos más tristes' indica sufrimiento; el superlativo intensifica el dolor emocional del hablante [1].

(b) 'Esta noche' sitúa el poema en un momento específico e inmediato [1]. Crea una sensación de urgencia y presente doloroso; el dolor no es abstracto sino concreto y actual, lo cual incrementa la intensidad lírica [1].

(c) 'Azules' — los astros azules evocan frialdad y distancia [1]. El color refuerza la soledad y el alejamiento de lo amado; no hay calidez en la noche, solo belleza fría e indiferente al sufrimiento del poeta [1].

(d) Los versos entre comillas funcionan como cita de una composición previa (metapoesía) [1]. Sugiere que el poeta ha intentado expresar su dolor antes; la distancia entre él y sus propios versos refleja la dificultad de capturar el sufrimiento auténtico con el lenguaje poético [1].

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt: Analiza el uso del tiempo y la memoria en Cien años de soledad de Gabriel García Márquez. En tu respuesta, haz referencia a al menos dos episodios específicos de la novela.
markScheme:
Estructura temporal circular:
• La novela comienza con Melquíades profetizando lo que el lector lee, y termina con el apocalipsis de Macondo — el tiempo es circular, no lineal [1]
• El primer párrafo ('Muchos años después, frente al pelotón de fusilamiento...') establece una temporalidad anticipatoria que desafía la narración cronológica [1]

La pérdida de la memoria:
• La plaga del insomnio y el olvido — los habitantes de Macondo pierden la memoria y deben etiquetar objetos con su nombre y función; ilustra cómo la identidad depende de la memoria colectiva [1–2]

Episodios específicos:
• La masacre de las bananeras: la versión oficial borra el recuerdo del evento; José Arcadio Segundo es el único testigo pero nadie le cree — la memoria colectiva puede ser manipulada [1–2]
• Los pergaminos de Melquíades: la profecía escrita contiene la historia completa de los Buendía; el tiempo ya está escrito, sugiriendo la inexorabilidad del destino [1]

Significado temático:
• El olvido y la repetición de los nombres (José Arcadio, Aureliano) reflejan la condena de la familia y de América Latina a repetir sus errores históricos [1]

Max 8 puntos

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt: En Bodas de sangre de Lorca, explica cómo la figura de la Luna y la Muerte (personificadas) contribuyen al carácter trágico de la obra. Incluye citas o referencias a escenas concretas.
markScheme:
Función de la Luna personificada:
• En el Acto III, la Luna aparece como leñador con cara blanca — forma parte del coro simbólico lorquiano que anuncia el destino fatal [1]
• La Luna activamente busca la sangre de los amantes: 'Que no escapen. Que no escapen.' — no es un elemento pasivo sino un agente de muerte [1]

Función de la Mendiga (Muerte):
• La Mendiga es una personificación velada de la Muerte, que guía a Leonardo y al Novio hacia su encuentro final [1]
• Su presencia en el bosque hace que el realismo ceda ante el mito y el símbolo; el desenlace ya no es contingente sino cosmológicamente necesario [1]

Carácter trágico:
• Lorca utiliza estas figuras para elevar el conflicto personal (honra, pasión) al nivel del mito griego [1]
• El honor, la sangre y el destino son fuerzas tan irresistibles como los dioses en la tragedia clásica [1]

Referencias concretas:
• La canción de la Luna en Acto III ('La luna deja un cuchillo / abandonado en el aire') — imagen de la luna como asesina [1]
• El simbolismo del cuchillo que reaparece a lo largo de la obra anticipa el desenlace violento [1]

Max 8 puntos

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:
Lee el siguiente fragmento de «Juana de América» de Juana de Ibarbourou:

«Tómame ahora que aún es temprano
y que llevo dalias nuevas en la mano.
Tómame ahora que aún es sombría
esta taciturna cabellera mía.»

Analiza tres recursos literarios presentes en el fragmento y explica su efecto.
markScheme:
Por cada recurso: [1] identificación, [1] efecto (máx. 3 recursos = 6 puntos)

Anáfora ('Tómame ahora... / Tómame ahora'):
• La repetición crea insistencia y urgencia; el imperativo 'tómame' revela un sujeto lírico que exige ser amado en su plenitud juvenil antes de que pase [1+1]

Imagen sensorial ('dalias nuevas en la mano'):
• Las flores frescas simbolizan la juventud y la vitalidad; 'nuevas' refuerza la idea de un momento fugaz que hay que aprovechar [1+1]

Epíteto ('taciturna cabellera'):
• La oscuridad del cabello connota misterio y también el tiempo presente, antes de que el tiempo la cambie; lo 'taciturno' introduce una nota de melancolía que contrasta con la vitalidad de las flores [1+1]

Max 6 puntos

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:
Identifica el tipo de narrador en cada una de las siguientes obras y explica cómo afecta la perspectiva narrativa al lector:

(a) Cien años de soledad (García Márquez) [3 puntos]
(b) El Aleph (Borges) [3 puntos]
markScheme:
(a) Narrador omnisciente en tercera persona:
• El narrador conoce pensamientos, sentimientos e historia de todos los personajes y de toda la saga Buendía [1]
• La omnisciencia crea una perspectiva casi divina o mítica — el narrador parece conocer el destino de Macondo desde el principio [1]
• Genera una distancia épica que convierte la historia familiar en saga histórico-mítica de América Latina [1]

(b) Narrador autodiegético (protagonista) en primera persona:
• El narrador-personaje ('Borges') relata su propia experiencia de ver el Aleph [1]
• La primera persona crea ambigüedad: ¿es confiable el narrador? Su obsesión con Beatriz y su envidia de Carlos Argentino pueden distorsionar lo que narra [1]
• La ironía del texto reside en que el narrador poco confiable intenta describir lo que es por definición indescriptible — el punto desde el que se ve todo [1]

Max 6 puntos

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:
Lee el siguiente fragmento de La casa de los espíritus de Isabel Allende y realiza un comentario guiado:

«Clara anotaba en su cuaderno los hechos importantes, los sueños proféticos, las recetas de cocina y las cartas nunca enviadas, para dominar la realidad y preservar los recuerdos antes de que la realidad quedara definitivamente atrás.»

(a) ¿Qué función narrativa cumple el cuaderno de Clara en la novela? [2 puntos]
(b) Analiza la enumeración ('hechos importantes, los sueños proféticos, las recetas de cocina y las cartas nunca enviadas'). ¿Qué sugiere esta combinación? [3 puntos]
(c) Explica el significado de la frase 'dominar la realidad'. [3 puntos]
markScheme:
(a) El cuaderno de Clara es el origen del manuscrito que Esteban Trueba y su nieta Alba reconstruyen; es un dispositivo metaficcional que justifica el acto de narrar [1]. También funciona como símbolo de la memoria femenina preservada frente a la historia oficial dominada por los hombres [1].

(b) La enumeración mezcla lo grande y lo pequeño, lo sobrenatural y lo doméstico [1]. Los 'sueños proféticos' y las 'recetas de cocina' aparecen al mismo nivel — Allende sugiere que todos los aspectos de la vida femenina son igualmente dignos de registro y preservación [1]. Las 'cartas nunca enviadas' introducen una nota de comunicación frustrada, de voces silenciadas que la escritura puede recuperar [1].

(c) 'Dominar la realidad' implica que escribir es un acto de poder: quien controla el relato controla la verdad [1]. Para Clara, con sus poderes clarividentes, la escritura es también una forma de domesticar lo profético, de convertir lo intangible en permanente [1]. Temáticamente, la novela explora cómo la escritura femenina desafía el poder masculino que pretende borrar la historia de los vencidos [1].

Max 8 puntos

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt: Compara el tratamiento del tema del poder en La casa de los espíritus (Allende) y La casa de Bernarda Alba (Lorca). ¿Cómo utilizan los dos autores el espacio cerrado (la casa) para representar la opresión?
markScheme:
El poder en Allende:
• Esteban Trueba ejerce poder patriarcal sobre su familia, sus inquilinos y eventualmente apoya la dictadura militar [1]
• La casa de los Trueba es escenario de violencia doméstica y también de resistencia — Clara y sus hijas construyen un espacio alternativo de autonomía femenina [1]

El poder en Lorca:
• Bernarda ejerce un poder matriarcal tiránico sobre sus ocho hijas después de la muerte de su segundo marido [1]
• La casa está literalmente cerrada ('¡Silencio!'); las ventanas dan al exterior pero las hijas no pueden salir — la arquitectura simboliza la represión [1]

El espacio cerrado como metáfora:
• En ambas obras la casa es una prisión: en Lorca, de las convenciones sociales y el honor; en Allende, del patriarcado y la dictadura [1]

• 'Sentí vértigo y lloré' — la experiencia de la totalidad no produce éxtasis sino terror y desolación [1]

subject: Literatura Española A
prompt:
Lee el siguiente fragmento del «Romance sonámbulo» de García Lorca (Romancero gitano) y realiza un comentario guiado:

«Verde que te quiero verde.

El barco sobre la mar

(a) Identifica y analiza el recurso retórico del primer verso. [3 puntos]
(b) Explica el contraste entre los dos últimos versos y su efecto. [3 puntos]
(c) ¿Qué función cumple la repetición del color verde en el contexto del poema? [2 puntos]
markScheme:
(a) Políptoton y anáfora en «Verde que te quiero verde»:
• El mismo adjetivo se usa en distintas funciones gramaticales (adjetivo y sujeto implícito); también funciona como anáfora al iniciarse y concluir el verso con 'verde' [1]
• El efecto es de encantamiento, hipnosis — el ritmo circular del verso crea una musicalidad obsesiva [1]
• Lorca también usa la sinestesia emocional: el verde no es solo visual sino afectivo ('que te quiero verde') — asocia el color con el deseo [1]

(b) El contraste entre «el barco sobre la mar» (agua, fluidez, horizonte abierto) y «el caballo en la montaña» (tierra, fuerza, movimiento terrestre):
• Son imágenes que normalmente pertenecen a mundos distintos pero se yuxtaponen sin conector lógico [1]
• El efecto es onírico — la lógica del sueño domina el poema; las imágenes flotan sin necesitar explicación [1]
• El barco y el caballo también simbolizan libertad y movimiento, contrastando con la quietud melancólica del poema [1]

(c) El verde en el Romancero gitano representa lo sobrenatural, lo mágico, lo deseado pero inalcanzable [1]. Es el color de la muchacha gitana que espera en el balcón — el verde de su piel y sus ojos mezcla vida, deseo y muerte (verde de enfermedad, de misterio) [1].
